questions.question: What does a collaborative-document backend have to solve before 'use WebSockets' becomes meaningful?
options: How edits converge, how versions advance, and how reconnecting clients catch up safely.
Which monospace font the editor ships with.
How to render comments in the browser sidebar.
Whether the document title is limited to 80 characters.
questions.explanation: Transport alone does not define correctness for concurrent editing.

questions.question: Why are snapshots useful in collaborative systems with operation logs?
options: They bound replay cost so reconnecting or recovering clients do not reapply an unbounded edit history.
They make presence heartbeats unnecessary.
They remove the need for durable operation logs.
They guarantee perfect conflict resolution.
questions.explanation: Operation history is useful, but replay must stay bounded.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of routing a document session through an owner or coordinator?
options: It simplifies sequencing and convergence reasoning for live edits.
It ensures all users are always in one geographic region.
It eliminates hotspot risk for popular documents.
It removes the need for snapshots.
questions.explanation: A session owner can make ordering and merge rules easier to reason about.

questions.question: Which metric best reveals that replay cost is becoming a user problem?
options: Reconnect recovery time or replay length per active document.
Average toolbar button count.
Number of document titles beginning with a vowel.
Median comment character length.
questions.explanation: Replay pain becomes visible when recovery time grows.

questions.question: Why should presence usually be treated as ephemeral state?
options: Because stale cursor or online indicators are tolerable compared with corrupting document truth.
Because presence has no user value.
Because presence can never be stored in memory.
Because presence only matters for offline editing.
questions.explanation: Presence is important, but it should not outrank document correctness.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with using a central sequencer or owner per active document?
options: It makes convergence easier but can create hotspots for highly active documents.
It guarantees every client can edit offline for days without merge work.
It removes the need for snapshots and logs.
It makes transform bugs impossible.
questions.explanation: Simplicity on correctness often costs concentration of load.

questions.question: What is a realistic way to detect corruption in transform or merge logic?
options: Replay operations deterministically against snapshots and compare resulting state.
Assume client cursors will reveal corruption immediately.
Disable concurrent editing in production after every deploy.
Store no versions so mismatches cannot be observed.
questions.explanation: Deterministic validation is one of the few concrete safety checks available.

questions.question: What changes most when the product adds true offline editing with long reconnect gaps?
options: Merge strategy, conflict semantics, and replay design become significantly more complex.
Presence heartbeats become the only critical subsystem.
Snapshots become unnecessary because clients have local copies.
The service can drop durable history entirely.
questions.explanation: Long-lived divergence expands the convergence problem dramatically.
